title: CBD Office Tower Property Manager
setting: A premium multi-tenant office tower in a major central business district
companySize: Large institutional ownership with specialist support teams
description: You manage a high-profile office tower where tenant expectations are sharp, owner reporting is disciplined, and building services cannot wobble for long without reputational and financial consequences. Your day sits at the intersection of tenant experience, contractor control, budgets, and client trust.
salary: Often mid-five to low-six figures base in major markets, with higher ranges for premium assets and more complex portfolios.

schedule.activity: Review overnight incidents, security notes, and open work orders before the building fills up
schedule.note: The best time to catch service risk is before tenants feel it.

schedule.activity: Walk the lobby, amenities, loading dock, and key common areas with engineering or facilities support
schedule.note: A ten-minute walk often tells you more than an hour of desk reporting.

schedule.activity: Tenant-service triage: HVAC comfort issue, after-hours access request, and one fitout noise complaint
schedule.note: Fast acknowledgment protects confidence even when fixes take longer.

schedule.activity: Monthly owner reporting prep: review occupancy, arrears, capex status, and budget variance commentary
schedule.note: Client trust grows when the story behind the numbers is already clear.

schedule.activity: Contractor review meeting for cleaning and HVAC performance
schedule.note: Vendors shape tenant experience as much as the property team does.

schedule.activity: Working lunch with leasing support to review one upcoming expiry and one vacancy strategy question
schedule.note: Property management and leasing work best when they share timing and market context.

schedule.activity: Client call with ownership representative to discuss a lift-modernization capex case and one tenant relationship issue
schedule.note: Owners want judgment, not just status updates.

schedule.activity: Review fitout progress on a new tenant floor and confirm handover risk items
schedule.note: Handover problems become operational problems very quickly.

schedule.activity: Reset tomorrow's priority stack and document follow-ups for tenants, contractors, and finance
schedule.note: A calm close of day protects the next morning from avoidable chaos.

responsibilities: Maintain service quality and building presentation for a high-visibility office asset
Coordinate vendors, engineering teams, and fitout stakeholders
Prepare monthly client reporting and budget commentary
Support lease obligations, renewals, and tenant relationship management
Escalate incidents and protect compliance, safety, and owner confidence
Shape capex recommendations for aging-plant and tenant-experience upgrades
challenges: Premium-office tenants have low tolerance for comfort, lift, and cleanliness issues
Owner reporting standards are high and often fast-moving
A single building issue can affect many tenants at once
Fitout and access coordination can disrupt normal service rhythm
There is constant pressure to balance service quality with expense control
rewards: You develop strong client-facing judgment on a visible commercial asset
The work is varied and combines operations, finance, and stakeholder management
You gain experience that translates well into portfolio or senior client roles
Strong performance is highly visible to owners and internal leadership
You learn quickly because the building gives constant real-world feedback
careerPath: Property coordinator or assistant property manager
Senior property manager on premium office assets
Head of property operations, asset-management support, or account leadership
